turns.content: I'm back in the game after five years away from Rust and I'm trying to understand how a no_std bootloader actually loads an application into RAM. Specifically, if I have my linker script defining memory regions for flash and SRAM, how does the boot process ensure the application code is placed at exactly the offset the CPU expects?

The core concept you're missing is that a bootloader isn't just Rust code sitting in memory; it's an assembly or C program that sets up the hardware state before handing control to your Rust application. You need to think of this as a two-stage boot process where the first stage is the bootloader itself, which runs from flash and performs initialization like setting up the stack pointer and clearing global variables used by the no_std environment. The bootloader then jumps to the address defined in your linker script for the main application binary. So when you're writing your Rust crate with cfg!(feature = "bootloader") enabled, you're actually building a separate binary that gets linked into the same ELF file as the application but loaded at a different memory address by the bootloader's jump instruction. Does this distinction between the bootloader binary and the application binary make sense to you?

I think I see where my mistake is coming from, but I was assuming the Rust compiler would handle all the memory layout automatically like in std environments. I don't understand how the linker script actually maps out the flash regions for both the bootloader and the application without causing a collision or overwriting critical data. Can you explain more concretely what goes wrong if my linker script doesn't properly separate these regions?

The common mistake is forgetting that in no_std environments, there's no automatic memory management or garbage collection; everything must be explicitly defined in your linker script using the .section directive to carve out specific areas for code and data. If you don't define a distinct section for the bootloader binary, the linker will place it right next to your application code, causing them to overwrite each other when loaded into flash. For instance, if your application expects its code at 0x1000 but the bootloader occupies that space or extends beyond it, the CPU will execute garbage data instead of valid instructions. You need to ensure your linker script explicitly reserves a region for the bootloader section before defining your application's sections so they never overlap in memory. What specific error message are you seeing when you try to build this combined binary?

I'm getting a relocation error saying that symbols from the bootloader are being referenced at wrong addresses, which suggests my linker script isn't handling the jump properly. I tried using .section with the bootloader name but didn't account for how the CPU actually jumps between these regions during boot. How do I ensure the jump is valid and doesn't cause a crash when transferring control?

The relocation error happens because the linker assumes everything starts at offset zero, but in your case, you're trying to load two binaries into the same file with different entry points and memory layouts. You need to explicitly define separate sections for flash and data in your linker script, ensuring the bootloader section ends before the application section begins. Then, when you link both the Rust crate (compiled with no_std features) and the assembly bootloader into a single ELF, the loader will correctly place them at their respective offsets. The key is that the jump instruction in the bootloader must point to the exact address where your Rust binary starts after compilation, which you calculate based on your linker script's output. Have you tried using a linker script file with explicit memory map definitions for flash and SRAM?

I have a linker script but I'm not sure how to properly align my sections so the application loads at the correct offset without corrupting the bootloader or leaving uninitialized code in memory. Is there a specific way to handle the alignment of the Rust crate's output within the no_std context when combined with an assembly bootloader?

When compiling your Rust crate for embedded use, you must explicitly set the linker flags to ensure proper alignment and placement relative to your bootloader sections. You should compile your application binary with a specific entry point address that matches what your bootloader expects it to jump to, which is defined in your linker script's memory map. The Rust compiler output will be placed at the offset specified by .section .text { ALIGN(4) } directives in your linker script, ensuring that when the bootloader jumps, it lands exactly on valid code rather than padding or garbage data. You also need to ensure that your no_std crate doesn't assume any global variables exist before initialization, so the linker script must reserve enough space for those variables after the application is loaded but before the main function runs. What specific alignment requirements does your hardware architecture impose?
I'm trying to understand the difference between a bootloader and an application in no_std contexts, specifically regarding how they handle memory maps.

Great question. The key distinction is that the bootloader acts as a bridge that initializes hardware before handing control to the application. In terms of the linker script, you define separate sections for the bootloader and your main firmware, ensuring they don't overlap in the flash memory layout.
I see, but isn't this just a standard function call? If I just jump to my app's entry point after setting up registers, why do we need that complex linking setup instead of a simple function pointer?

You've hit on the core reason. A simple function pointer assumes a specific memory layout that might not exist or be safe to trust when booting from flash. The bootloader must explicitly configure the stack, initialize peripherals like timers and interrupts, and set up the vector table before jumping to your application's entry point.
So if I forget to call `init()` on my system clock or GPIO during that setup phase, will my app just crash immediately?

Exactly. If you skip initialization steps like setting up the stack pointer or configuring the clock tree, your application might fail to run correctly because it assumes a specific hardware state. The bootloader's job is to ensure those prerequisites are met before execution begins.
That makes sense, but what if I want to update my firmware later without re-flashing the entire chip? How does that fit into this architecture?

Ah, you're asking about over-the-air updates. In that scenario, your bootloader needs to support a dual-mode design where it can store both a current running image and a backup image in separate memory regions of the flash map. It verifies the integrity of the new firmware before jumping to it, allowing for safe upgrades without losing existing data.

I've been thinking about how this no_std environment relates to my Linux kernel development, where I use the memory map and boot arguments. It feels like I'll just need to write a tiny bootloader that sets up the stack and jumps to the application binary, similar to how the kernel handles early boot. Is it really that simple compared to setting up pages in the kernel?

It's not quite as simple as your Linux experience suggests because you won't be managing full virtual address spaces or page tables at this stage; instead, you're dealing with raw physical memory and a specific memory map layout defined by the board. The key difference is that in no_std, you don't get a standard runtime environment to help you handle initialization, so your bootloader must manually configure the stack pointer, set up the heap using something like alloc::Heap or a custom allocator trait implementation, and ensure the linker script correctly places the firmware image at the right offset. You also need to be careful about calling functions that require std features because in no_std environments only safe code is allowed unless you explicitly enable unsafe blocks.

Ah, so I was thinking I could just link my application directly without any special setup like a bootloader, assuming the linker would handle it automatically. That seems too easy compared to how I manage boot arguments in Linux. Can't I just write Rust code and let the compiler do everything?

You cannot skip the bootloader step because the linker alone cannot perform hardware initialization; it only arranges symbols in memory based on your input files, but it doesn't know how to execute the code or set up the CPU registers. In a no_std context, you must explicitly implement the boot process by writing assembly or Rust that sets up the stack and jumps to your application entry point, often using a trait like `core::convert::From` for the startup logic if you're transitioning from std. Without this manual setup, the processor won't know where to begin execution, and you risk corrupting memory or failing to initialize critical peripherals before your main logic runs.

Okay, so I need to write a separate assembly file to jump into my Rust application, right? And then how does that relate to updating firmware later when the bootloader needs to handle re-flashing? It feels like I'm adding complexity just to make it run. Why can't I assume the OS will take care of this for me?

You are correct that you must write a separate assembly file or use a Rust crate designed for bootloading, such as `bootloader` or `no_std_firmware`, because no_std environments do not provide an operating system to manage memory and execution flow. The reason the OS doesn't handle this is that embedded systems lack a standard runtime; they rely on direct hardware access through traits like `core::ptr::read_unaligned` for reading registers directly from the memory map. When you later implement firmware updates, the bootloader will use these same low-level mechanisms to overwrite specific regions of the flash or RAM, but it must be careful not to corrupt your application's symbol table or break the linker script layout.

I see, so the memory map is critical here because if I mess up where my Rust code ends and the bootloader begins, the jump will fail. Does that mean I have to manually verify every byte in the flash before flashing? It seems like a lot of work compared to how Linux handles updates.

You don't need to manually verify every byte in the flash; instead, you can use tools like `flashrom` or specific linker scripts configured for your board to ensure the binary is written correctly. The critical part is ensuring that the no_std code respects the memory boundaries defined by your linker script so that when the bootloader jumps to the application, it lands at a valid address without overlapping with its own data sections. This is why you must carefully define the entry point and stack size in Rust using traits like `core::panic::PanicInfo` only if you need runtime error handling, but primarily focus on getting the symbol addresses correct so the jump instruction points to exactly where your application starts.

This makes sense. So my Rust code needs to know its own location and size so I can safely jump into it from the bootloader without corrupting anything. It's a bit more manual than Linux but necessary for this environment. Thanks for clarifying how no_std requires explicit memory management compared to having an OS handle everything.

Exactly, in no_std you must explicitly manage memory and execution flow because there is no hidden layer like the Linux kernel providing default services; you are responsible for configuring registers, setting up the stack, and ensuring your application binary is placed correctly in the memory map before the bootloader jumps to it. This manual control allows you to create custom bootloaders that can handle complex scenarios like A/B updates or secure boot verification, but it requires precise coordination between your Rust code, linker script, and assembly entry points.

I'm trying to port my Python script that uses ctypes to a Rust bootloader, but I thought since we're using the `core::mem` module and standard library traits like `impl Drop`, it should just work automatically without needing any linker scripts or custom memory maps. Can't I just link against `std` and let the compiler handle everything?

That's a great starting point, but there's a fundamental misunderstanding here: Rust's embedded ecosystem is designed to run where the standard library isn't available by default. When you write code that assumes `std::mem::forget` exists or uses `impl Drop` on types like `String`, you're relying on runtime heap allocation and garbage collection which bootloaders often don't have access to yet. The correct approach is to target a no_std environment from the start, meaning you need to explicitly configure your linker script to define the memory layout for your bootloader section before it jumps to the application code.

Ah, so I can't just assume `core::mem` is enough? Like when I was writing my Python wrapper around a C function, I didn't think about how much stack space that call actually consumed. But in Rust, isn't there some automatic memory management via the `impl Drop` trait that handles cleanup without needing manual stack checks?

The `impl Drop` trait is crucial for managing resources like file handles or network sockets, but it doesn't automatically manage raw stack space for your entire program. In a bootloader context, you need to manually define how much memory the linker will use for your code and data sections using something like `.section .text { ... }` in your linker script, because there's no global allocator running at startup yet. You're essentially building a custom memory map that tells the system where your bootloader goes versus where the application starts.

Okay, so I need to write my own linker script manually? That sounds complicated compared to just importing crates from `cargo`. Is there a way to avoid writing raw assembly or manually defining every byte of stack usage, since Rust usually abstracts that away with things like `#[no_mangle]` for symbols?

While Rust provides macros and traits to manage symbol visibility via `#[no_mangle]`, it doesn't replace the need for explicit linker configuration in no_std environments. You absolutely cannot skip defining your own memory map because the bootloader needs to know exactly where to place its code relative to the application's entry point. There are crates like `bootloader` or `gdbstub` that help, but they still require you to provide a custom linker script that defines the stack and heap regions properly before the system can jump to your application code.

Wait, so even if I use those helper crates, I'm still manually specifying how much stack my bootloader uses? That feels like I'm back to doing things I thought Rust would handle automatically. Is there a way to tell `cargo` to do this for me without writing any assembly or linker script lines at all?

Not really; Rust's build system doesn't automatically generate memory maps for you in no_std contexts because the exact placement depends on your hardware architecture and specific bootloader requirements. You'll need to define a custom linker script that specifies where your code goes, how much stack you allocate, and what address the application should jump to after the bootloader finishes its job. This is why no_std projects often feel more manual than full-std crates—there's less abstraction when dealing with bare metal constraints.

Got it, so I can't just plug in a crate and expect automatic memory management like in my Python scripts where `ctypes` handled everything for me. But once I get the linker script right, does that mean I can then safely jump to my application code without worrying about stack overflow or uninitialized variables?

Exactly—once your linker script correctly defines the memory regions and you've properly initialized your stack pointer before jumping, you can safely transition to your application code. The key is ensuring your bootloader doesn't overwrite any critical data structures that the application expects, which is why careful attention to the memory map and proper use of traits like `impl Copy` for small constants helps prevent corruption during that handoff moment.

That makes sense, so my next step should be defining a custom linker script with explicit stack and heap regions, then making sure my bootloader initializes everything correctly before jumping. Thanks for clarifying how no_std changes the game compared to what I thought Python gave me automatically!

You're very welcome! Just remember that while Rust gives you more control than interpreted languages, it still requires explicit setup for bare-metal environments. If you run into issues with your linker script, checking the generated symbol table or using a debugger to verify the actual memory layout can help catch problems early.

I'm reviewing the new firmware project and it seems to be a pure no_std bootloader. I've always treated this like my old ARM assembly bootloaders where I manually calculated offsets in the .text section, but here everything is handled by the linker script and the Rust compiler itself.

That's exactly how it works. In a no_std environment you can't rely on heap or standard libraries so the bootloader must manage memory manually using the linker script to define the .text section and stack, then use the start function entry point that the CPU jumps to after reset.
So when I look at the symbol table there's a _start symbol but no malloc or std::main. How does the linker know where to jump without any runtime initialization?

The linker script defines a specific start address in the memory map and the Rust compiler generates code at that location using the _start symbol which is the entry point for the no_std binary. The linker ensures the .text section begins exactly where the CPU expects it to be after the reset vector.
I see a problem though. If I add a function that needs stack space like a custom error handler, will the compiler automatically reserve enough space in the .bss section?

Not necessarily. The Rust compiler assumes standard heap behavior which doesn't exist here. You must explicitly allocate stack frames or use unsafe blocks to manage memory manually, otherwise you'll get a linker error because the symbol table won't match your actual memory layout.
So if I try to call a standard library function like println! it will fail immediately since there's no std?

Yes, any attempt to use std::fmt or similar traits will cause a compile error. You must implement your own formatting logic using the core::fmt trait and write custom output handlers that work directly with the UART registers.

I tested this by adding a panic handler but it didn't trigger as expected. Turns out the panic implementation in no_std doesn't use the default stack unwinding and I had to manually set up an exception handler that jumps to the reset vector.

That's a critical gotcha. In no_std environments the panic implementation is minimal and often just halts execution, so you must explicitly implement a custom panic hook that writes to flash or UART before resetting the system.
